rationale: The cited paper is used repeatedly as a core anchor for the employee-based brand equity literature, not just as background. The citing paper directly builds hypotheses from it (e.g., prior evidence that stronger brands can lower pay) and also adopts related operational choices (such as brand-to-firm mapping logic and log-pay treatment). It then explicitly qualifies that prior account by arguing that only vertical differentiation lowers pay, while horizontal differentiation can increase pay, and by disputing the implied profit benefit of lower pay.
relation_description: The citing paper takes Tavassoli et al. (2014) as a key prior foundation on brand effects in labor markets, then sharpens and qualifies its implications. It separates vertical vs. horizontal brand differentiation and shows different pay and profit pathways, including that leveraging vertical differentiation to suppress pay can hurt profits via productivity and retention.
